title: Lipschitz Graphs in Carnot Groups
speaker: Davide Vittone
abstract: Submanifolds with intrinsic Lipschitz regularity in Carnot groups (i.e.,stratified groups endowed with a sub-Riemannian structure) can beintroduced using the theory of intrinsic Lipschitz graphs started yearsago by B. Franchi, R. Serapioni and F. Serra Cassano. One of the mainrelated questions concerns a Rademacher-type theorem about the almosteverywhere existence of a tangent plane to intrinsic Lipschitz graphs:

proof_stages.label: Carnot and Heisenberg ambient geometry
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes the ambient class by defining stratified/Carnot groups, their dilations and homogeneous distances, and then singles out the Heisenberg groups as the principal noncommutative model.

proof_stages.label: Intrinsic Lipschitz graphs and Rademacher problem
proof_stages.summary: Using complementary homogeneous subgroups, the speaker defines intrinsic graphs and the cone condition, formulates intrinsic differentiability by blow-up to a homogeneous subgroup, states the a.e. differentiability theorem in Heisenberg groups, and records Lusin, rectifiability, and area-formula consequences.

proof_stages.label: Extension, approximation, and level-set tools
proof_stages.summary: The proof is reduced to entire graphs by an extension theorem and to smooth intrinsic Lipschitz graphs by an approximation theorem, both relying on an equivalent level-set characterization with a coercive horizontal derivative.

proof_stages.label: Rumin currents in Heisenberg groups
proof_stages.summary: Rumin's complex of Heisenberg differential forms and its associated notion of current and boundary are introduced as the intrinsic integration theory needed for the proof.

proof_stages.label: Boundaryless graph current and tangent multivector
proof_stages.summary: Smooth approximating graphs define boundaryless currents converging to a current supported on the original graph, represented by integration against a measurable multivector field tau whose Lebesgue points are proposed as differentiability points.

proof_stages.label: First blow-up and verticality
proof_stages.summary: At a Lebesgue point of tau, every graph blow-up carries a boundaryless limiting current with constant multivector tau-bar, and the codimension-one theory is invoked to ensure that almost every such blow-up is vertically invariant.

proof_stages.label: Second blow-up and constant-current rigidity
proof_stages.summary: Vertical invariance makes the first blow-up Euclidean Lipschitz, so blowing up again gives planes carrying constant-current limits; a Heisenberg constant-coefficient current theorem forces the plane to be uniquely determined by tau-bar, yielding a unique blow-up and intrinsic differentiability.

proof_stages.label: Failure of step-two generalization
proof_stages.summary: A counterexample in H^1 x R, built from a 1/2-Hölder function with nonconvergent parabolic difference quotients, shows that intrinsic Lipschitz Rademacher and C^1-rectifiability statements can fail in general step-two Carnot groups.

examples.name: Euclidean product splitting and classical graph
examples.note: The Euclidean decomposition R^n=R^k x R^{n-k} and the usual graph of a Lipschitz map are used only to make the intrinsic splitting and graph definitions familiar.
examples.engagement_reason: he uses the Euclidean product splitting as the standard analogy for the intrinsic graph definition, but does not compute or verify anything on it

examples.name: Euclidean level-set test for a Lipschitz graph
examples.note: The classical statement that a graph in the last coordinate direction can be detected by a Lipschitz level-set function with coercive last derivative is presented as a model for the Carnot-group level-set characterization.
examples.engagement_reason: he analyzes the Euclidean level-set characterization, rejects naive versions via the distance-function/eikonal observation, and formulates the coercive directional-derivative test

passes.reason: The Euclidean coercive-vertical-derivative level-set criterion is a standard graph test used as an analogy for the Carnot formulation.

examples.name: Cantor-type half-Hölder function picture
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker sketches a self-similar staircase construction that supplies the required 1/2-Hölder function for the step-two counterexample, but the picture is not needed for the Heisenberg Rademacher proof.
examples.engagement_reason: he describes the Cantor-type half-Hölder construction step by step with scaled copies and the upside-down middle piece, and relates the limit to the desired non-differentiability property

passes.reason: The Cantor-type 1/2-Hölder function is scaling-critical and is used to build an intrinsic-Lipschitz map with nowhere intrinsic differentiability.
The Cantor-type critical half-Hölder function is used to build an intrinsic Lipschitz graph where Rademacher differentiability fails.
The Cantor-type half-Hölder function is a critical nowhere-fractionally-differentiable construction used to make Rademacher differentiability fail.
The Cantor-type function is critical one-half Hölder, matching the weight-two vertical scaling, and is used to build an intrinsic Lipschitz map for which Rademacher differentiability fails.
The Cantor-type critical one-half-Hölder function is used to build an intrinsic Lipschitz graph where Rademacher differentiability fails.
The Cantor-type critical 1/2-Hölder function is used to build an intrinsic Lipschitz graph where Rademacher differentiability fails everywhere.
